source_paper_title: Variational Inequalities on Unbounded Domains for Zero-Sum Singular Controller vs. Stopper Games
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Bovo_2025, title={Variational Inequalities on Unbounded Domains for Zero-Sum Singular Controller vs. Stopper Games}, volume={50}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.0029}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.0029}, number={1}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Bovo, Andrea and De Angelis, Tiziano and Issoglio, Elena}, year={2025}, month=feb, pages={277–312} }
context:
Page 7, Section 3 (Remark 3.5).

Uniqueness of the solution to Problem A remains an open question. Methods used in, e.g., [35], do not apply due to the presence of obstacle and gradient constraints.
title: Uniqueness of Sobolev solutions to min-max variational inequality with obstacle and gradient constraints
background_latex:
Fix a finite horizon \(T\in(0,\infty)\) and dimension \(d\in\mathbb{N}\). Let \(b:\mathbb{R}^d\to\mathbb{R}^d\) and \(\sigma:\mathbb{R}^d\to\mathbb{R}^{d\times d'}\) be continuously differentiable, locally Lipschitz, with at most linear growth, and let \(a(x):=\sigma(x)\sigma(x)^\top\). Assume local ellipticity: for every bounded \(B\subset\mathbb{R}^d\) there exists \(\theta_B>0\) such that \(\langle \zeta, a(x)\zeta\rangle\ge \theta_B\|\zeta\|_d^2\) for all \(x\in B\), \(\zeta\in\mathbb{R}^d\).

Define the second-order operator acting on sufficiently regular \(\varphi\) by
\[
(L\varphi)(x)=\tfrac12\mathrm{tr}\big(a(x)D^2\varphi(x)\big)+\langle b(x),\nabla \varphi(x)\rangle.
\]
Let \(r\ge 0\) be a constant. Let \(f,g,h:[0,T]\times\mathbb{R}^d\to[0,\infty)\) be continuous, with the regularity and growth conditions ensuring the expressions below are well-defined almost everywhere for Sobolev functions: specifically assume \(g\) and \(f^2\) are locally \(C^{1,2,\alpha}\) and \(h\) is locally \(C^{0,1,\alpha}\) for some \(\alpha\in(0,1)\), and \(g(t,x)+h(t,x)\) has at most quadratic growth in \(x\). Assume also the compatibility condition
\[
\|\nabla g(t,x)\|_d\le f(t,x),\qquad (t,x)\in[0,T]\times\mathbb{R}^d.
\]

For \(p>d+2\), consider functions \(u\in W^{1,2,p}_{\mathrm{loc}}([0,T]\times\mathbb{R}^d)\). By Sobolev embedding, such \(u\) have a locally H\"older-continuous spatial gradient, so the pointwise constraints \(u\ge g\) and \(\|\nabla u\|_d\le f\) are meaningful.

Define the (parabolic) min-max variational inequality on \([0,T)\times\mathbb{R}^d\) with terminal condition \(u(T,x)=g(T,x)\):
\[
\min\Big\{\,\max\big\{\partial_t u + Lu - r u + h,\; g-u\big\},\; f-\|\nabla u\|_d\,\Big\}=0\quad \text{a.e. on }[0,T)\times\mathbb{R}^d.
\]
A solution is understood in the strong (Sobolev) sense, i.e. the PDE terms hold almost everywhere, with \(u\) satisfying the obstacle and gradient constraints and the terminal condition.
problem_definition_latex:
Determine whether the above min-max variational inequality with terminal condition \(u(T,x)=g(T,x)\) admits at most one solution \(u\in W^{1,2,p}_{\mathrm{loc}}([0,T]\times\mathbb{R}^d)\) (for some/every \(p>d+2\)) satisfying the quadratic-growth bound \(|u(t,x)|\le c(1+\|x\|_d^2)\) for a constant \(c>0\).

Equivalently, prove or disprove: if \(u_1,u_2\in W^{1,2,p}_{\mathrm{loc}}([0,T]\times\mathbb{R}^d)\) both satisfy
1) \(u_i(T,x)=g(T,x)\) for all \(x\in\mathbb{R}^d\),
2) \(u_i\ge g\) and \(\|\nabla u_i\|_d\le f\) pointwise on \([0,T]\times\mathbb{R}^d\),
3) \(\min\{\max\{\partial_t u_i + Lu_i - r u_i + h,\; g-u_i\},\; f-\|\nabla u_i\|_d\}=0\) a.e. on \([0,T)\times\mathbb{R}^d\),
4) \(|u_i(t,x)|\le c(1+\|x\|_d^2)\),
then \(u_1\equiv u_2\) on \([0,T]\times\mathbb{R}^d\).
latex:
Fix a finite horizon \(T\in(0,\infty)\) and dimension \(d\in\mathbb{N}\). Let \(b:\mathbb{R}^d\to\mathbb{R}^d\) and \(\sigma:\mathbb{R}^d\to\mathbb{R}^{d\times d'}\) be continuously differentiable, locally Lipschitz, with at most linear growth, and let \(a(x):=\sigma(x)\sigma(x)^\top\). Assume local ellipticity: for every bounded \(B\subset\mathbb{R}^d\) there exists \(\theta_B>0\) such that \(\langle \zeta, a(x)\zeta\rangle\ge \theta_B\|\zeta\|_d^2\) for all \(x\in B\), \(\zeta\in\mathbb{R}^d\).

Define the second-order operator acting on sufficiently regular \(\varphi\) by
\[
(L\varphi)(x)=\tfrac12\mathrm{tr}\big(a(x)D^2\varphi(x)\big)+\langle b(x),\nabla \varphi(x)\rangle.
\]
Let \(r\ge 0\) be a constant. Let \(f,g,h:[0,T]\times\mathbb{R}^d\to[0,\infty)\) be continuous, with the regularity and growth conditions ensuring the expressions below are well-defined almost everywhere for Sobolev functions: specifically assume \(g\) and \(f^2\) are locally \(C^{1,2,\alpha}\) and \(h\) is locally \(C^{0,1,\alpha}\) for some \(\alpha\in(0,1)\), and \(g(t,x)+h(t,x)\) has at most quadratic growth in \(x\). Assume also the compatibility condition
\[
\|\nabla g(t,x)\|_d\le f(t,x),\qquad (t,x)\in[0,T]\times\mathbb{R}^d.
\]

For \(p>d+2\), consider functions \(u\in W^{1,2,p}_{\mathrm{loc}}([0,T]\times\mathbb{R}^d)\). By Sobolev embedding, such \(u\) have a locally H\"older-continuous spatial gradient, so the pointwise constraints \(u\ge g\) and \(\|\nabla u\|_d\le f\) are meaningful.

Define the (parabolic) min-max variational inequality on \([0,T)\times\mathbb{R}^d\) with terminal condition \(u(T,x)=g(T,x)\):
\[
\min\Big\{\,\max\big\{\partial_t u + Lu - r u + h,\; g-u\big\},\; f-\|\nabla u\|_d\,\Big\}=0\quad \text{a.e. on }[0,T)\times\mathbb{R}^d.
\]
A solution is understood in the strong (Sobolev) sense, i.e. the PDE terms hold almost everywhere, with \(u\) satisfying the obstacle and gradient constraints and the terminal condition.

Determine whether the above min-max variational inequality with terminal condition \(u(T,x)=g(T,x)\) admits at most one solution \(u\in W^{1,2,p}_{\mathrm{loc}}([0,T]\times\mathbb{R}^d)\) (for some/every \(p>d+2\)) satisfying the quadratic-growth bound \(|u(t,x)|\le c(1+\|x\|_d^2)\) for a constant \(c>0\).

Equivalently, prove or disprove: if \(u_1,u_2\in W^{1,2,p}_{\mathrm{loc}}([0,T]\times\mathbb{R}^d)\) both satisfy
1) \(u_i(T,x)=g(T,x)\) for all \(x\in\mathbb{R}^d\),
2) \(u_i\ge g\) and \(\|\nabla u_i\|_d\le f\) pointwise on \([0,T]\times\mathbb{R}^d\),
3) \(\min\{\max\{\partial_t u_i + Lu_i - r u_i + h,\; g-u_i\},\; f-\|\nabla u_i\|_d\}=0\) a.e. on \([0,T)\times\mathbb{R}^d\),
4) \(|u_i(t,x)|\le c(1+\|x\|_d^2)\),
then \(u_1\equiv u_2\) on \([0,T]\times\mathbb{R}^d\).
open_problem_quote: Uniqueness of the solution to Problem A remains an open question. Methods used in, e.g., [35], do not apply due to the presence of obstacle and gradient constraints.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: Non-uniqueness (or uniqueness) is a universal analytical property of a PDE/variational inequality on an unbounded continuous domain; there is no obvious finite, discretizable instance family whose verification would constitute a rigorous counterexample without additional structure.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: A coding-agent loop could explore discretizations or restricted parametric families of coefficients/payoffs to search numerically for multiple solutions or to test candidate comparison principles, but translating numerical evidence into a rigorous uniqueness proof/counterexample would still require substantial human-led analysis.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
In the source paper (Bovo–De Angelis–Issoglio, arXiv:2203.06247v3) the value \(v\) of a finite-horizon zero-sum singular-controller vs. stopper game on \([0,T]\times\mathbb R^d\) is identified as the maximal strong Sobolev solution \(u\in W^{1,2,p}_{\mathrm{loc}}\) (\(p>d+2\)) of the min–max variational inequality \(\min\{\max\{\partial_t u+Lu-ru+h,\ g-u\},\ f-\|\nabla u\|\}=0\) with terminal condition \(u(T,\cdot)=g(T,\cdot)\) and quadratic growth. The paper explicitly leaves open whether such a Sobolev solution is unique (Remark 3.5), noting that standard approaches (e.g. those used for singular control without an obstacle, or obstacle problems without gradient constraints) do not directly yield a comparison principle in the presence of both hard constraints and the min–max structure.

Forward-citing work to date provides substantial partial progress but no definitive uniqueness theorem for the original locally elliptic problem on \(\mathbb R^d\). Two strands are prominent. First, several papers establish uniqueness/maximality for approximating or regularised problems where uniform ellipticity is restored or the gradient constraint is modified: for example, “Stopper vs. Singular controller games with degenerate diffusions” proves that for each uniformly elliptic perturbation \(a_\varepsilon=a+\varepsilon^2I\) there is a (unique/maximal) strong Sobolev solution to the corresponding regularised VI, and then studies the \(\varepsilon\downarrow0\) limit, but stops short of proving that the limit satisfies the original VI in strong Sobolev sense or that uniqueness holds in the locally elliptic/degenerate setting. Similarly, “Zero-Sum Stopper Versus Singular-Controller Games with Constrained Control Directions” develops an approximation \(\gamma\downarrow0\) for direction-constrained controls, obtaining maximal strong solutions for approximating VIs and convergence of values, but again without a limiting strong-Sobolev VI characterisation or uniqueness.

Second, a set of one-dimensional works on the half-line or time-homogeneous models (“Finite-time horizon, stopper vs. singular-controller games on the half-line”, “On the saddle point of a zero-sum stopper vs. singular-controller game”, and “Global regularity of the value function in a stopper vs. singular-controller game”) provide refined regularity and free-boundary structure (e.g. global \(C^1\) regularity, smooth-fit at the control boundary, and probabilistic representations for \(v_x\)). These results strengthen the analytical toolbox in special 1D settings and show that maximality can coincide with strong regularity and explicit boundary characterisations, but they still do not deliver a general comparison principle for the multi-dimensional Sobolev VI with variable \(f(t,x)\) and obstacle \(g(t,x)\). Overall, the uniqueness problem remains open; the most promising directions appear to be (i) developing a comparison principle tailored to the coupled obstacle/gradient-constraint min–max operator under quadratic growth on \(\mathbb R^d\), or (ii) proving that the maximal Sobolev solution coincides with the game value and is stable under penalisation/regularisation in a way that forces uniqueness (e.g. via monotone approximation and tightness/compactness arguments that rule out multiple strong solutions).

sources.title: Stopper vs. Singular controller games with degenerate diffusions
sources.theorem_or_result:
For each 3>0, the perturbed (uniformly non-degenerate) stopper–singular controller game has a value function u_3\in W^{1,2,p}_{\mathrm{loc}}([0,T]\times\mathbb{R}^d) (any p\ge1) which is the maximal strong Sobolev solution (with linear/quadratic growth) of the min–max variational inequality
\[
\min\{\max\{\u2202_t u+L_\u00c03 u-r u+h,\ g-u\},\ f-|\u2207 u|\}=0,\qquad u(T,\u00c0x)=g(T,\u00c0x),
\]
where \(L_\u00c03\) has diffusion matrix \(a_\u00c03(x)=a(x)+\u00c03^2 I\) (uniform ellipticity). As \(\u00c03\to0\), \(u_\u00c03\to v\) (the value of the original possibly degenerate game) uniformly on compacts (and with an explicit rate under additional regularity), and \(v\) is continuous and satisfies the gradient bound \(|\u2207 v(t,\u00c0x)|\le f(t)\) a.e.
sources.connection:
The open problem asks for uniqueness of strong Sobolev solutions \(u\in W^{1,2,p}_{\mathrm{loc}}\) to the stated min–max variational inequality on \([0,T)\times\mathbb{R}^d\) under local ellipticity and quadratic growth. This paper does not prove such a uniqueness theorem. Instead it:
- Uses the earlier uniformly elliptic theory (their Theorem 6, citing [1]) to obtain, for each \(\u00c03>0\), a unique/maximal Sobolev solution \(u_\u00c03\) to a *regularized* VI with \(a_\u00c03=a+\u00c03^2 I\).
- Lets \(\u00c03\downarrow0\) to construct a value function \(v\) for the degenerate game, but explicitly states they do not obtain a variational-inequality characterization of \(v\) in the degenerate case.
Thus, relative to the open problem, the paper provides partial progress/related evidence: it confirms well-posedness (existence and a maximal Sobolev characterization) in the uniformly elliptic approximations and develops approximation/compactness-free probabilistic techniques, but it does not establish uniqueness for the original locally elliptic Sobolev VI on \(\mathbb{R}^d\) with obstacle and gradient constraint.

sources.title: Finite-time horizon, stopper vs. singular-controller games on the half-line
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proves (Theorem 2.4) existence of a value for a 1D finite-horizon zero-sum stopper vs. singular-controller game on the half-line 

- state space 
\(O=[0,T)\times(0,\infty)\) with absorption at \(x=0\),
- payoff with running cost \(h\), terminal payoff \(g\), and singular-control cost \(\bar\alpha\,d|\nu|\),
- controlled diffusion \(dX_t=\mu(X_t)dt+\sigma(X_t)dW_t+d\nu_t\).

It shows the value \(v\) is the maximal Sobolev solution \(u\in W^{1,2,p}_{\mathrm{loc}}(O)\cap C(\bar O)\) (for \(p>3\)) of the variational inequality with obstacle and gradient constraint plus boundary/terminal conditions:
\[\min\{\max\{\partial_t u+Lu-ru+h,\ g-u\},\ \bar\alpha-|\partial_x u|\}=0\ \text{a.e. on }O,\quad u(T,x)=g(T,x),\ u(t,0)=g(t,0).\]
Uniqueness is established for the penalised semilinear PDE approximations (Corollary 3.3) and for the penalised problem on the unbounded domain (via probabilistic representation, Proposition 4.7), but the limiting variational inequality is characterised as having a maximal solution rather than being proved unique.
sources.connection:
The open problem asks for uniqueness of strong (Sobolev) solutions on \([0,T]\times\mathbb R^d\) to a min-max variational inequality with both an obstacle \(u\ge g\) and a gradient constraint \(\|\nabla u\|\le f\), under local ellipticity and quadratic growth.

This citing paper treats a closely related min-max variational inequality but only in the 1D half-line setting \([0,T]\times[0,\infty)\) with an additional Dirichlet boundary condition at \(x=0\) and with constant gradient bound \(|u_x|\le \bar\alpha\) (so \(f\) is constant). It proves existence of a value and identifies it with the maximal Sobolev solution of that VI. However, it does not prove that the Sobolev solution is unique: the main PDE statement is maximality, and the uniqueness results shown are for penalised approximating PDEs, not for the limiting VI itself. Therefore it provides partial progress (existence/characterisation and new estimates) but does not resolve the uniqueness question posed in full generality (nor even in this 1D domain case).

sources.title: Zero-Sum Stopper Versus Singular-Controller Games with Constrained Control Directions
sources.theorem_or_result:
For each approximation parameter \(\gamma\in(0,1]\), the associated unconstrained-direction game admits a value \(u^\gamma\) which is the maximal strong Sobolev solution \(u^\gamma\in W^{1,2,p}_{\mathrm{loc}}([0,T]\times\mathbb{R}^d)\) (for all \(p\)) of the min–max variational inequality
\[
\min\{\max\{\partial_t u+Lu-ru+h,\ g-u\},\ f^\gamma-|\nabla u|_\gamma\}=0,\qquad u(T,\cdot)=g(T,\cdot),
\]
with linear growth; moreover \(u^\gamma\to v\) as \(\gamma\downarrow0\) uniformly on compacts, where \(v\) is the value of the constrained-direction game, and \(v\) satisfies the partial gradient bound \(|\nabla_0 v(t,x)|\le f(t)\) a.e. and admits an optimal stopping rule \(\theta^*=\tau^*\wedge\sigma^*\).
sources.connection:
The open problem asks for uniqueness of strong (Sobolev) solutions \(u\in W^{1,2,p}_{\mathrm{loc}}\) with quadratic growth for the original min–max variational inequality with obstacle \(u\ge g\) and full gradient constraint \(|\nabla u|\le f(t,x)\) on \([0,T)\times\mathbb{R}^d\). This paper does not prove such a uniqueness theorem. Instead it:
- Uses the earlier paper [4] (the source of the open problem) to obtain existence/characterisation and maximality for *approximating* VIs with a modified norm \(|\cdot|_\gamma\) and smoother/bounded data.
- Passes \(\gamma\downarrow0\) to obtain existence of a game value \(v\) in the constrained-control-direction setting, but explicitly notes they cannot guarantee solvability of the limiting VI in strong Sobolev sense.
- Provides convergence and stability estimates and an optimal stopping time, which are relevant tools/insight for future PDE/VI analysis, but do not establish comparison/uniqueness for the Sobolev VI in the open problem’s generality (notably: their main assumptions include sublinear growth and \(f=f(t)\), and the limiting object is not shown to be a Sobolev solution of the original VI).
Hence it is partial progress/related, not a solution.

sources.title: On the saddle point of a zero-sum stopper vs. singular-controller game
sources.theorem_or_result:
In a 1D, time-homogeneous zero-sum stopper vs. singular-controller game on  or [0,\infty), the value function \(v\) belongs to \(W^{1,2,p}_{\mathrm{loc}}([0,T]\times O)\) for all \(p\in[1,\infty)\), satisfies the associated obstacle/gradient-constrained variational inequalities (as in (2.6)) with quadratic growth, and admits a saddle point \((\tau^*,\nu^*)\). The saddle point is characterised by two monotone moving boundaries \(a(t)\) (stopping) and \(b(t)\) (control), with \(\tau^*=\inf\{s\ge0: X^{\nu}_s\le a(t+s)\}\wedge(T-t)\) and \(\nu^*\) given by Skorokhod reflection at \(b\). A key auxiliary result is a probabilistic representation of the spatial derivative \(v_x\) as the value of an optimal stopping problem with absorption:
\[
v_x(t,y)=\inf_{\tau}\,\mathbb E\Big[e^{-\int_0^\tau \lambda(Y_u)du}\,\alpha_0\mathbf 1_{\{\tau<\tau_a\}}+\int_0^{\tau\wedge\tau_a} e^{-\int_0^s \lambda(Y_u)du}\,h_x(t+s,Y_s)\,ds\Big],
\]
with \(\tau_a\) the hitting time of the stopping boundary \(a\) by an auxiliary diffusion \(Y\), and \(\lambda=r-\mu_x\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for uniqueness of strong (Sobolev) solutions to a parabolic min-max variational inequality with obstacle \(u\ge g\) and gradient constraint \(\|\nabla u\|\le f\) on \([0,T)\times\mathbb R^d\), under local ellipticity and quadratic growth. This paper does not address uniqueness in that general PDE setting. It works in a specialised 1D, time-homogeneous game with constant gradient bound \(f\equiv\alpha_0\) and obstacle depending only on time \(g(t)\), and focuses on constructing a saddle point and free-boundary structure assuming existence/characterisation results from earlier work ([6,7]). While it derives additional regularity/structure (monotone boundaries, representation of \(v_x\)), it does not prove a comparison principle or uniqueness for Sobolev solutions of the min-max variational inequality, neither in multi-D nor even explicitly in the 1D PDE class. Thus it provides partial progress/related structural tools rather than solving the uniqueness question.

sources.title: Global regularity of the value function in a stopper vs. singular-controller game
sources.theorem_or_result: In the one-dimensional finite-horizon stopper vs. singular-controller game with constant gradient constraint \,\(v_x\le \alpha_0\) and obstacle \,\(v\ge g(t)\), the value function \,\(v\) satisfies the associated min–max variational inequality and enjoys global \,\(C^1\)-regularity: \,\(v\in C([0,T]\times O)\cap C^1([0,T)\times O)\). Moreover, \,\(v_x\in C^1(C\cup \mathrm{int}(S))\), and the second spatial derivative \,\(v_{xx}\) and mixed derivative \,\(v_{tx}\) are continuous across the control boundary \,\(b(t)\) (inaction/action boundary), with smooth-fit \,\(v_{xx}(t,b(t)-)=0\), while \,\(v_{xx}\) may jump across the stopping boundary \,\(a(t)\) (Theorem 3.8, Theorems 6.2–6.4, Proposition 5.2).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for uniqueness of strong (Sobolev) solutions \,\(u\in W^{1,2,p}_{\mathrm{loc}}\) to a parabolic min–max variational inequality on \([0,T)\times\mathbb R^d\) with obstacle \,\(u\ge g\) and gradient constraint \,\(\|\nabla u\|\le f\), under quadratic growth. This paper does not prove such a uniqueness theorem. Instead it studies a specific 1D game (\(d=1\)) with a constant gradient cap \,\(f\equiv \alpha_0\) and an obstacle depending only on time \,\(g=g(t)\), and establishes higher regularity (global \(C^1\)) of the value function and fine boundary regularity (smooth-fit at the control boundary). These results are compatible with, and could be useful for, uniqueness arguments in that special 1D setting (e.g., enabling verification/Itô formulas), but they do not address uniqueness of Sobolev solutions in the general \(d\)-dimensional, variable \(f(t,x)\), variable obstacle \(g(t,x)\) framework posed in the open problem.
